article.title: Study of the genetic variability of durum wheat (Triticum durum Desf.) in the face of combined stress: water and heat

events.event_label: water reduces wheat yield
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p003.s001: The devastating effects and extent of abiotic stress on cereal production continue to increase globally, affecting food security in several countries, including Tunisia. 10781440.p003.s002: Heat waves and the scarcity of rainfall strongly affect durum wheat yields. 10781440.p003.s003: The present study aims to screen for tolerance to combined water and heat stresses in durum wheat at the juvenile stage. 10781440.p003.s004: Three combined treatments were tested, namely: T0 (100% field capacity (FC) at 24 °C), T1 (50% FC at 30 °C), and T2 (25% FC at 35 °C).

events.event_label: abiotic stresses reduce yield and quality
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p008.s001: Drought and heat stresses are the major factors limiting wheat productivity and yields. 10781440.p008.s002: Indeed, the two abiotic stresses cause reductions in the number and weight of grains, inducing significant losses in the yield and quality of cereals.

events.event_label: stress decreases photosynthetic efficiency (Fv/0)
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p029.s001: The results revealed that the photosynthetic efficiency (Fv/0) decreased under combined stress compared to the control. 10781440.p029.s002: Under moderate stress (T1), Fv/0 decreased by 28.02% compared to a decrease of 37.22% under severe stress (T2) compared to the control (T0) (Fig. 1B).

events.event_label: stress decreases chlorophyll content
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p032.s001: Chlorophyll a and b content tends to decrease under combined stress. 10781440.p032.s002: Thus, under moderate stress (T1), the chlorophyll content decreased by 30.1%, compared to a decrease of 44.9% under severe stress (T2) (Fig. 1C). 10781440.p032.s003: Moreover, only the genotypes Karim and Hmira showed a significant decrease in Chla under both stress levels (T1 and T2) (Fig. 1D). 10781440.p032.s004: Interestingly, the Chla/Chlb ratio decreased for all the tested genotypes except for the genotype Karim.

events.event_label: stress decreases Chla in Karim and Hmira
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p032.s001: Chlorophyll a and b content tends to decrease under combined stress. 10781440.p032.s002: Thus, under moderate stress (T1), the chlorophyll content decreased by 30.1%, compared to a decrease of 44.9% under severe stress (T2) (Fig. 1C). 10781440.p032.s003: Moreover, only the genotypes Karim and Hmira showed a significant decrease in Chla under both stress levels (T1 and T2) (Fig. 1D). 10781440.p032.s004: Interestingly, the Chla/Chlb ratio decreased for all the tested genotypes except for the genotype Karim.

events.event_label: stress decreases relative water content
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p033.s001: A general trend of decreasing RWC was noted in the durum wheat genotypes studied (Fig. 1E). 10781440.p033.s002: Indeed, the maximum reduction in RWC was recorded under severe stress (T2) (67.6%), against a decrease of 37.58% under moderate stress (T1), compared to T0. 10781440.p033.s003: Importantly, the genotype Karim possesses the most remarkable reduction in RWC (77.2%) compared to other genotypes under severe combined stress (T2) (Fig. 1E).

events.event_label: stress increases leaf temperature
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p034.s001: Genotypic variability was observed for LT, with a general trend of increase under combined stress compared to control (T0) (Fig. 1F). 10781440.p034.s002: Under moderate stress (T1), LT increased by 16.97% compared to an increase of 26.03% under severe stress (T2) compared to control (T0) (Fig. 1F). 10781440.p034.s003: The highest mean LT was observed in the genotypes Hmira and Karim at 38.8 °C and 38.1 °C, respectively (Table 1).

events.event_label: stress increases proline content
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p037.s001: The variation in PC was significantly (P < 0.001) affected by the different treatments applied (Table 1). 10781440.p037.s002: Thus, PC tends to increase under the effect of combined stress. 10781440.p037.s003: The maximum increase was recorded under severe stress (T2) with 154.6% and under moderate stress (T1) with 92.2%, compared to the control (T0). 10781440.p037.s004: The results showed that the genotype Hedhba has the highest PC (209.1%) under the two stress combinations, followed by Aouija with 199.6% (Fig. 1H).

events.event_label: RWC positively correlates with photosynthetic efficiency traits
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p039.s001: Correlation analysis (Fig. 2) highlighted positive correlations recorded between RWC and the chlorophyll fluorescence traits Fv/m and Fv/0 (r = 0.91***, r = 0.64***, respectively). 10781440.p039.s002: Also, a positive correlation was observed between RWC and Chla (r = 0.69***) and Chlb (r = 0.86***), confirming the importance of RWC in the components of photosynthesis mechanisms under harsh environmental conditions. 10781440.p039.s003: Interestingly, RWC was shown to be negatively correlated with the osmo-regulators, which tend to increase under stress conditions, SSC and PC (r = −0.74*** and r = −0.46). 10781440.p039.s004: Moreover, Fv/m was positively correlated with Fv/0, Chla, and Chlb (r = 0.65***, r = 0.69***, r = 0.8***).

events.event_label: negative correlations between physiological parameters under combined stress
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p039.s009: LA was negatively correlated with PC (r = −0.55***). 10781440.p039.s010: Negative correlations were recorded between Fv/m and LT (r = −0.88***) (Fig. 2). 10781440.p039.s011: As well, Chlb was negatively correlated with SSC (r = −0.63***) (Fig. 2).

events.event_label: stress decreases leaf area
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p035.s001: Abiotic stress caused a reduction in LA of 16.20% and 41.4% under moderate (T1) and severe (T2) stress, respectively. 10781440.p035.s002: The greatest decrease was observed under severe stress (T2) for Biskri (57.3%) and Aouija (32.9%) (Table 1).

events.event_label: drought and heat stress induce leaf senescence leading to chlorophyll degradation and reduced yield
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p054.s001: In addition, drought and heat stress can induce leaf senescence, leading to chlorophyll degradation and photosynthesis disruption, which hamper crop yield. 10781440.p054.s002: Chlorophyll is the main pigment for photosynthesis and is most sensitive to high temperatures and water stress. 10781440.p054.s003: High temperatures destroy chlorophyll and damage plants by reducing light quantum acceptance. 10781440.p054.s004: Similarly, chlorophyll concentrations decreased more in plants exposed to combined drought and heat stress than individual stresses in wheat.

events.event_label: combined drought/heat stress elevates osmolytes (proline, glycine, betaine, soluble sugars) contributing to osmotic adjustment
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p059.s001: To survive combined drought/heat stress, plants change their metabolism to promote the synthesis of osmolytes and secondary metabolites that promote stress tolerance. 10781440.p059.s002: Plants affected by drought and heat stress accumulate compatible solutes, such as proline, glycine, betaine and soluble sugars, which play a role in osmotic adjustment to maintain water status and protect leaf cells. 10781440.p059.s003: Osmotic adjustment allows the plant to maintain turgor pressure at low water potential, which is important for maintaining metabolic functions. 10781440.p059.s004: The results showed that the genotypes Hedhba and Aouija showed the highest proline accumulation.

events.event_label: High temperatures decrease chlorophyll and RWC
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p052.s001: Our results showed that the lack of water accompanied by high temperatures induced a significant decrease in RWC, LA, and chlorophyll rate, which is in agreement with the findings of. 10781440.p052.s002: This situation fosters more accelerated senescence and leaf abscission. 10781440.p052.s003: Indeed, a reduction in the hydration of durum wheat induces a decrease in biomass. 10781440.p052.s004: Plant water status is highly dependent on thermal stability.

events.event_label: Combined drought and heat stress affect chlorophyll fluorescence and PSII activity
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p055.s001: On the other hand, combined drought and heat stress affect chlorophyll fluorescence in various crops, reducing or even stopping PSII activity due to a reduced concentration of photosynthetic pigments. 10781440.p055.s002: For example, a combined drought/heat stress (35/20 °C; 40–45% FC) applied for 15 days significantly reduced chlorophyll fluorescence values in 12 wheat varieties.

events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p057.s001: In addition, Fv/m and Fv/0 are considered important parameters to assess the integrity of internal mechanisms in the leaf during photosynthetic activities and are considered an accurate method for screening plant tolerance to water stress. 10781440.p057.s002: Our results showed that these parameters are strongly affected by the applied stresses (Table 1).

events.event_label: water and heat stresses affect vegetative organs

events.event_label: combined stress reduces photosynthetic activity, affecting production and yield
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p061.s001: Our results show that the five durum wheat genotypes use the same strategies to tolerate combined stress, and emphasize the accumulation of single stresses that induced remarkable damage illustrated by the low growth and the sharp reduction in photosynthetic activity which will also influence production and yield later.

events.event_label: abiotic stress (T1, T2) reduces leaf area

events.event_label: combined stress (moderate and severe) increases PC
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p037.s001: The variation in PC was significantly (P < 0.001) affected by the different treatments applied (Table 1). 10781440.p037.s002: Thus, PC tends to increase under the effect of combined stress. 10781440.p037.s003: The maximum increase was recorded under severe stress (T2) with 154.6% and under moderate stress (T1) with 92.2%, compared to the control (T0). 10781440.p037.s004: The results showed that the genotype Hedhba has the highest PC (209.1%) under the two stress combinations, followed by Aouija with 199.6% (Fig. 1H).

events.event_label: heat stress and severe stress increase soluble sugar content
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p060.s001: Under conditions of water and heat stress, plants accumulate other osmotically active substances like sugars. 10781440.p060.s002: Our data showed a substantial increase in this solute correlated with the severity of applied stress, as previously reported. 10781440.p060.s003: The increase in SSC was higher under the treatments at (T2) than under (T1) and this trend was particularly remarkable for Aouija than for the other genotypes. 10781440.p060.s004: Sensitive plants showed less increase in SSC than tolerant plants.

events.event_label: Water stress and combined stress repress electron transport and enhance non‑photochemical quenching
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p056.s001: In addition, chlorophyll fluorescence analysis revealed that applied stress results in a significant decrease in Fv/m for all studied genotypes. 10781440.p056.s002: This decrease can be explained by a reduction in the transfer of electrons to the reaction centre of the PSII due to variations in energy absorption, electron trapping, transport and dissipation per section, leading to a reduction in photosynthetic efficiency of PSII. 10781440.p056.s003: According to, water stress followed by heat stress progressively decreases electron transport from PSII and increases non-photochemical quenching in wheat leaves that support the role of alternative sink electrons (from either PSII or PSI) and the flow of cyclic electrons for the photoprotection of PSII and PSI, which also generate the ATP needed to cope with combined stress conditions. 10781440.p056.s004: In this sense, the chlorophyll fluorescence of the genotype Aouija was the least affected.

events.event_label: water deficit and high temperature reduce Fv/m and inhibit PSII photochemistry
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p058.s001: A reduction in Fv/m by water deficit and high temperature suggests a possible inhibition of PSII photochemistry, which may be due to an inadequate relocation of the energy of the light-harvesting chlorophyll complex towards the reaction centre. 10781440.p058.s002: A minimal decrease in Fv/m was observed for Aouija indicating that higher defensive capacity for PSII is a key resistance mechanism for durum wheat landraces, which is in agreement with previous studies for durum wheat accessions under water and heat stress conditions. 10781440.p058.s003: For the parameters reported in this work, Aouija is more tolerant to abiotic stress and could be an opportunity for breeding programs. 10781440.p058.s004: It is important to remember that these morpho-physiological parameters were defined by low rates of decrease in the genotypes Aouija and Hedhba, which suggests that the latter two are tolerant to combined stress despite the accumulation of stress effects.

events.event_label: combined drought/heat stress increases osmolytes and metabolites
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p059.s001: To survive combined drought/heat stress, plants change their metabolism to promote the synthesis of osmolytes and secondary metabolites that promote stress tolerance. 10781440.p059.s002: Plants affected by drought and heat stress accumulate compatible solutes, such as proline, glycine, betaine and soluble sugars, which play a role in osmotic adjustment to maintain water status and protect leaf cells. 10781440.p059.s003: Osmotic adjustment allows the plant to maintain turgor pressure at low water potential, which is important for maintaining metabolic functions. 10781440.p059.s004: The results showed that the genotypes Hedhba and Aouija showed the highest proline accumulation.

events.event_label: water deficit and high temperature inhibit PSII photochemistry
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p058.s001: A reduction in Fv/m by water deficit and high temperature suggests a possible inhibition of PSII photochemistry, which may be due to an inadequate relocation of the energy of the light-harvesting chlorophyll complex towards the reaction centre. 10781440.p058.s002: A minimal decrease in Fv/m was observed for Aouija indicating that higher defensive capacity for PSII is a key resistance mechanism for durum wheat landraces, which is in agreement with previous studies for durum wheat accessions under water and heat stress conditions. 10781440.p058.s003: For the parameters reported in this work, Aouija is more tolerant to abiotic stress and could be an opportunity for breeding programs. 10781440.p058.s004: It is important to remember that these morpho-physiological parameters were defined by low rates of decrease in the genotypes Aouija and Hedhba, which suggests that the latter two are tolerant to combined stress despite the accumulation of stress effects.

events.event_label: abiotic stresses are associated with durum wheat
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p009.s001: To cope with these constraints and to survive, plants have evolved different adaptation mechanisms by modifying their metabolic systems. 10781440.p009.s002: Currently, besides productivity, which has been the primary objective of most durum wheat improvement, breeding programs focus on the genetic improvement of abiotic stress tolerance (drought, salinity, cold, heat stress, etc.) through the identification of morpho-physiological and biochemical parameters. 10781440.p009.s003: Several physiological, morphological, and biochemical criteria have thus been identified. 10781440.p009.s004: Proline and soluble sugar accumulation, vegetative growth, and photosynthetic efficiency have been considered as tolerance criteria. 10781440.p009.s006: Most studies over the past decade have focused on crop responses to single stress. 10781440.p009.s007: However, crops often face a combination of stresses in the field. 10781440.p009.s008: In addition, drought and heat stress are often linked and concomitantly cause more losses than when they occur alone. 10781440.p009.s009: The effect of combined ‘drought/heat’ stress has been studied in several species at different levels: phenological, physiological and metabolic. 10781440.p009.s010: In the field, the combined ‘drought/heat’ stress causes a unique plant response that differs from single stresses.

events.event_label: abiotic stress and water affect crop production
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p003.s001: The devastating effects and extent of abiotic stress on cereal production continue to increase globally, affecting food security in several countries, including Tunisia. 10781440.p003.s002: Heat waves and the scarcity of rainfall strongly affect durum wheat yields. 10781440.p003.s003: The present study aims to screen for tolerance to combined water and heat stresses in durum wheat at the juvenile stage. 10781440.p003.s004: Three combined treatments were tested, namely: T0 (100% field capacity (FC) at 24 °C), T1 (50% FC at 30 °C), and T2 (25% FC at 35 °C).

events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p058.s001: A reduction in Fv/m by water deficit and high temperature suggests a possible inhibition of PSII photochemistry, which may be due to an inadequate relocation of the energy of the light-harvesting chlorophyll complex towards the reaction centre. 10781440.p058.s002: A minimal decrease in Fv/m was observed for Aouija indicating that higher defensive capacity for PSII is a key resistance mechanism for durum wheat landraces, which is in agreement with previous studies for durum wheat accessions under water and heat stress conditions. 10781440.p058.s003: For the parameters reported in this work, Aouija is more tolerant to abiotic stress and could be an opportunity for breeding programs. 10781440.p058.s004: It is important to remember that these morpho-physiological parameters were defined by low rates of decrease in the genotypes Aouija and Hedhba, which suggests that the latter two are tolerant to combined stress despite the accumulation of stress effects.

events.event_label: ROS positively_correlates_with leaf senescence
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p059.s003: Osmotic adjustment allows the plant to maintain turgor pressure at low water potential, which is important for maintaining metabolic functions. 10781440.p059.s004: The results showed that the genotypes Hedhba and Aouija showed the highest proline accumulation. 10781440.p059.s005: A high capacity for proline accumulation under water and/or heat stress is generally associated with high stress tolerance. 10781440.p059.s006: Increasing the production of these osmoprotectants decreases ROS production and reduces leaf senescence. 10781440.p059.s007: Proline is part of many stress signalling pathways involved in stress adaptation.

events.event_label: combined stress increases_abundance_of PC
events.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p064.s002: Indeed, the desiccation of the substrate induces a strong reduction in the RWC of the plant observed in Karim and Hmira followed by Biskri. 10781440.p064.s003: However, a significant increase in PC was recorded mainly in the genotype Hedhba followed by Aouija under the effects of combined stress.

relations.subject: water deficit

evidence.text: A reduction in Fv/m by water deficit and high temperature suggests a possible inhibition of PSII photochemistry, which may be due to an inadequate relocation of the energy of the light-harvesting chlorophyll complex towards the reaction centre.

relations.evidence_context_text: 10781440.p058.s001: A reduction in Fv/m by water deficit and high temperature suggests a possible inhibition of PSII photochemistry, which may be due to an inadequate relocation of the energy of the light-harvesting chlorophyll complex towards the reaction centre. 10781440.p058.s002: A minimal decrease in Fv/m was observed for Aouija indicating that higher defensive capacity for PSII is a key resistance mechanism for durum wheat landraces, which is in agreement with previous studies for durum wheat accessions under water and heat stress conditions. 10781440.p058.s003: For the parameters reported in this work, Aouija is more tolerant to abiotic stress and could be an opportunity for breeding programs. 10781440.p058.s004: It is important to remember that these morpho-physiological parameters were defined by low rates of decrease in the genotypes Aouija and Hedhba, which suggests that the latter two are tolerant to combined stress despite the accumulation of stress effects.

entities.selected_description: A pearson correlation coefficient calculation is a similarity calculation which attaches to each pair of random variables X and Y the ratio of their covariance by the product of their standard deviations. Given a series of n measurements of X and Y written as x_i and y_i where i = 1, 2, ..., n, then their Pearson correlation coefficient refers to the "sample correlation coefficient" and is written as the sum over i of the ratios (x_i-xbar)*(y_i-ybar)/((n-1)*s_x*s_y) where xbar and ybar are the sample means of X and Y , s_x and s_y are the sample standard deviations of X and Y. The closer the pearson correlation coefficient is to 1, the more similar the inputs are considered.

On the other hand, combined drought and heat stress affect chlorophyll fluorescence in various crops, reducing or even stopping PSII activity due to a reduced concentration of photosynthetic pigments.
For example, a combined drought/heat stress (35/20 °C; 40–45% FC) applied for 15 days significantly reduced chlorophyll fluorescence values in 12 wheat varieties.

This decrease can be explained by a reduction in the transfer of electrons to the reaction centre of the PSII due to variations in energy absorption, electron trapping, transport and dissipation per section, leading to a reduction in photosynthetic efficiency of PSII.

According to, water stress followed by heat stress progressively decreases electron transport from PSII and increases non-photochemical quenching in wheat leaves that support the role of alternative sink electrons (from either PSII or PSI) and the flow of cyclic electrons for the photoprotection of PSII and PSI, which also generate the ATP needed to cope with combined stress conditions.

In addition, Fv/m and Fv/0 are considered important parameters to assess the integrity of internal mechanisms in the leaf during photosynthetic activities and are considered an accurate method for screening plant tolerance to water stress.

Under conditions of water and heat stress, plants accumulate other osmotically active substances like sugars.

Our data showed a substantial increase in this solute correlated with the severity of applied stress, as previously reported. The increase in SSC was higher under the treatments at (T2) than under (T1) and this trend was particularly remarkable for Aouija than for the other genotypes.
Sensitive plants showed less increase in SSC than tolerant plants.

Our results show that the five durum wheat genotypes use the same strategies to tolerate combined stress, and emphasize the accumulation of single stresses that induced remarkable damage illustrated by the low growth and the sharp reduction in photosynthetic activity which will also influence production and yield later.
Water and heat stresses cause profound morpho-physiological and biochemical changes in the various vegetative organs involved in this study.
However, a significant increase in PC was recorded mainly in the genotype Hedhba followed by Aouija under the effects of combined stress.
